speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have a good day. I'll be back

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just remember to be quiet when you come in, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know this will be-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: The slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 10, 1996. I am interviewing Abraham Hetman, also known as Getman. My name is Barbara Hollander. We're conducting the interview in Long Beach, New York, in the United States, in English. I'm Barbara Hollander. I'm interviewing Mr Abraham Getman in Long Beach, New York. Today is July 10, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Getman, for allowing us into your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice present.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the name you were born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name is Abraham Getman. I was born by the Polish authority, government. And at that time, they called us 'Hetman.' And when the Russian occupied our part of Poland, and they-- they-- they changed the name to Getman, because, in Russian, the letter 'H' don't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In town of Luboml, in the Wolyn in Gubernia, that's-- and that's the northwest of Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And until 1939, was Poland. From '39 till '41, was occupied by the Russians. And in '41 till '44, by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And in what year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was born on July 21st, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Getman, can you tell me a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family? It's-- I had a-- a father and a mother, five brothers, and one sister. And in our house, our grandmother was living with us. And about two streets away, the parents from [BACKGROUND NOISE] my mother, my grandfather-- my grandmother and grandfather and a uncle were living on the same street. And this was-- the-- the name for the street, we called the Rynek. And my number was 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me-- let's start with your parents, so we get everyone's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Shmuel Hersh. My mother's named Miriam, my older brother, Yaakov, and Faigie, my sister, then my brother, Moshe, Chaim, and Leibel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a difference in two years apart from each of us. I was before the small-- the smallest, before Leibel, between Chaim and Leibel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents' name, from my-- my father's mother, Sorah-- Sorah, and my mother's father is Yitzchok. And the-- the grandmother, Margula--

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and a-- a brother, Moshe. And their last name was Zuckerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zuckerman. OK. So there were quite a few kids, in your home, close in age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been pretty lively in your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. And the same-- my mother had a sister, and who was-- their name was Faigy and her husband, Israel. And also had six children, three boys and three girls. And my-- this is from my mother's side. And my father had also sisters [BACKGROUND NOISE] and brothers, but two of them immigrate to United States, lived here in United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did you do, with your brothers and your sister, in your house for fun? What was it like living with all those boys, close in age, and one sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we was-- with the little brothers, we were fighting. With the older brothers, we-- we-- we had fun, go and play soccer, climb in trees, and going to some organizations for a picnic. And it was a very close family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you share a room with any of your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yes, we shared mostly three or four were in one room. And the sister had her own room. And the parents had a separate room. And my grandma-- and my grandmother had a separate room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your sister like the princess in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she like was the princess in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was your family a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a religious family, a Orthodox family, observed holy day, holidays. And even my father, oh, God, three times a day, praying. But [BACKGROUND NOISE] modern way, already, you know, not special dresses, modern clothes, modern-- like in the [INAUDIBLE]. And-- but the observant was strict and respectable [INAUDIBLE]. Like-- and my mother want to punish me, she sort of mention that, that your father come home, I'll tell him. This was punishment. So I mean-- but he never laid a hand on us. But for a-- so just for respect, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what sort of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a shoe store. He-- they used to tell us stories, how [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he survived a orphanage. And he had to support-- by 12 years old, he supports the-- he support the family. They lived in a village and when-- and bring in, to the market, straw and hay and other product to sell and everything. And also, his family background was a big family of six children. And then he became a businessman. And then he went to United States. And my father was there. And my grandfather was in the United States, too. They make money. And my grandfather talk him in to go back to marry his daughter, my-- my mother So they went back to Europe. This was in the First World War. Then he opened a shoe store. And we also, we had a-- he had a farm. And he support. The rest of the family were really poor in the villages. He was so supporting. And he was active in-- in charity places in our town. And I remember, because we had a shoe store, so the-- when the [BACKGROUND NOISE] joint distribution, they used to give him the money, and they trust him to buy shoes and a reasonable price, and they can-- they give out for the orphanage people, poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is-- her [NON-ENGLISH], I-- always, her house was-- she was the greatest cook. If I would have it now, something, I would believe this was manna. If you can eat so tasty food, which she cooked. And she was busy. You know six children to clean, there's not a-- you know, there's no showers, no out-- other facilities, [INAUDIBLE] facilities. Everything has to be done primitive. So she had a full hand. But we had-- well, it was good times, when Father made a good living. So he had the helps with the maids.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me about one holiday, maybe, that stands out in your mind, that your family celebrated together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every holiday was one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] Because, from one holiday to-- to others was a-- every time was a different type of food. So we knew what is coming. We were waiting for this occasion. And somehow this-- and we were waiting for this, because you get new clothes, new shoes, new dress. This special was for New Year's and Passover, just the two. Then Hanukkah, we were waiting to get the money, Hanukkah gelt. Purim, also the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] and entertainment, with [NON-ENGLISH]. People with orchestras come and knocking on the door and playing and begging for--for money, Purim gelt. It's a like [NON-ENGLISH], to march to the little forest for a picnic-- anything. Purim, to read the Megillah, with the graggers, some [NON-ENGLISH], with the flags, everything had a meaning. I'm telling you the truth. Here, in United States, by me, everything is one day. We don't know the difference between Shabbos is Sunday or Monday. There, it was a-- a feeling, a meaning. Even though I was young, 13 years old, I can't forget it. It's always with me in my mind. The Seder, Passover, sitting and chanting and singing. And I-- I can't describe. You have to be a special person to describe those all occasions. And it's another thing I really miss since-- since childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me about the school you went to, friends you had, et cetera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. School, we had a-- at that time, I went to public school. We didn't have buses. We have to walk, some for a half a mile or a mile. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And with-- the Jewish people went separate. Polish people had a separate school. Ukrainian had a separate school. Only by the end, before the war, they built one mixture-- mixed school. Otherwise, so we didn't have to go-- Saturday, we didn't have to go to the public school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a-- a-- a prayer. It was a National Prayer, but it didn't mention any religion. We have to thank-- and thank God for giving you this day, where the sun came out, what part the-- I don't. And I can't translate this, because it's in Polish. So-- and then we had certain-- in the school, we had sport, sport clubs for the soccer, volleyball, and everything. And each school competed with the other school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And before the war, they had one mixed school, and it was a brand new school. And-- and then-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] but the Russians was only-- they changed. They reduced the school, with a one grade lower, because their education was higher. So-- but otherwise, we had various social-- social like writing in a magazine, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] youth organization. We had also youth organizations, Zionist organizations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And was a soccer team in-- in the town, which were competing with little towns. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But this was cut off. When the Germans came-- came in, everything was cut off in this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No school. No friends. No anythings.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half our-- half our life was taken. And really, my best years was the-- not the teenager year. I didn't have a teenager years, all together--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --till age of 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. In your town, were there many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our town, there was about 6,0000 Jews. But in the-- in time from the war, they brought in, from the different villages, like in the ghettos, probably close to 10,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before the war, how were relations with the non-Jews, between the Jews and the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was anti-Semite. But-- but originally, was a friendship. Like we used to have a market. You know, the farmers used to bring their product into the-- the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the same farmers, they couldn't go home, it's night or storm, they would come into our house. We'll put straw on the floor and let them sleep-over with all the families.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody, every farmer had somebody in town he knew. And mostly of the people, where they licked-- lived in the little towns, they come from the villages. Because they went out to improve their life. So they know each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was only incidents, what we had, because we used to live in center in town. And we used to go out of town. And some of the non-Jews used to throw stones on us or rocks on us or bring the dogs, should bite us. We always-- so we mostly tried to go by groups and [LAUGHS] throw back stones and [? everything ?]. And-- and when you felt the occasions-- not from my time-- used to be pogroms. And my grandfather used to tell-- tell stories when pogroms would happen, but not for my time. But Polish time was they have Pilsudski. He was more liberal. So we had was given rights, like an a-- autonomy. For religious purposes, everybody was run by the-- by the Jewish people, the rabbis. We didn't have to go to court. They-- they let us settle. You-- you couldn't be a-- a policeman. They wouldn't take you for a policeman. Well, it was given to the Poles. It's not-- maybe I don't know. Because there probably is a job, but they wouldn't give it to-- to a Jew. It's a more of a dignitary job. But otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] for my town, wasn't that the problem. The problem was when it comes to the-- the war-- to war, when the propaganda with antisemitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, ordered by the Russian, they start to do this thing. Everything-- if somebody has trouble, they blame the Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK, and we're going to get to that more. When you look back on your childhood, what is your fondest memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] My memories, I-- is the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget it. I miss it. And I'm dreaming about this. And I always wish to have the same thing, to rebuild the family like for the olden time. You can't take away this feeling. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was the close nature. I-- I-- I-- I don't see it today, this-- the-- the relationship closeness. A cousin was a brother. A second cousin was also. It was like we were one. But I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this all was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. The Jewish community life, in your town, you talked a little bit about your father helping the poor people. But there were organizations set up to help the poor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There was organizations, and they had to have even schools for the-- Bais Yaakov, Talmud Torahs for poor people, where they didn't have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like we used to bake, like bake and cook. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Once Friday, they used to bake all week. So you have to leave so much and so much for poor people. And they-- after 12 o'clock, and this time, you made the-- the-- like the pita bread, so many, many pita bread you put away for people who come in. And then, like my grandmother, she-- and some people, they were ashamed to go begging. She used to wrap up and deliver it to them, to the houses, like challah and bread, other things. Everybody had something else to donate for Shabbos. They-- they see to it that-- that poor people should have the same enjoyable Shabbos, like the more wealthy people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so the town-- the Jews took care of each other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my-- my grandmother used to fast twice a week. Because two kids in my family-- before, I want to tell you-- [INAUDIBLE] died when the war-- in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, my older brother was sick. So my grandmother gave a neder, a-- a vow, that if he'll survive, she'll fast twice a week. So she was fasting on Monday and Thursday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I now realize-- because I am not really religious now, but now I'm going to the temple. I see like they- they read the Torah twice a week. Maybe this is the reason, where they picked those two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those two days, she's used to go collect money and give for the poor people. The same thing with my mother. And she has a friend, Feyge Shliva, across the street. She was a very dignified woman and all her-- good off. I cannot say, wealthy. [BACKGROUND NOISE] When there are people that had a little house, they are wealthy already. So if somebody needed help, in secrecy, so my mother used to go with this lady to collect money.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she went in to ask, usually, they-- in the small towns, they want to know, who, when? For who you giving it? They want to know everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when my mother, with this lady, used to go in asking for money, they never asked questions. They know it's somebody, it's an emergency. And they have to support.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a very charitable--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this lady died, I think, in 193-- '38, the other lady, a normal death. The whole city went to her funeral. And everybody was crying, for-- for-- for this lady, Feyge Shliva.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Moving into the early part of the war, what were the first signs of danger, that something was changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- it was in-- in 19 September, 1939. We knew there that Germany will do-- declare war. And we could see if in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] government is soliciting for money to buy weapons. And they were coming to our town, to the schools, even, for pennies to buy machine guns and everything. Because Hitler was threaten already, and the world was-- Czechoslovakia, Poland, it still was existing. And in Poland, we had our Marshal Smigly-Rydz. And he was so heroic. He said, we will fight the Germans. We wouldn't give up. Even a button from our coats, we wouldn't give up. We'll fight. And we'll win. And nobody believed that after two weeks, Poland was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] invaded and destroyed. But the meantime, it was the Ribbentrop with Molotov agreement that a part of Poland, we will give to the Russians. And the front was-- it happened to be, this border is by our town. The Bug River is-- They call us, Western Ukraine. We belong to-- in what it was Eastern Poland, Western Ukraine. We'll give them to the Russian. But the Germans, they came in to our town anyway. And we were afraid. We were scared. The Ukrainian farmers came with bags, sacks under their arms, to rob the stores. Because they knew the Germans don't like the Jews, and they'll give them a free hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happened. The Germans came in, and the opposite. She-- they gave out orders, everybody will be punished. Anybody who will break into stores or to houses will be punished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they let us know that this part, [BACKGROUND NOISE] we have agreement. They are only temporary. In a couple days, that the Russian army will come in here. In the same time, the farmers left back to their homes. In the same time, there was a lot of communist people were waiting already for liberation with this. They went and to make a gates to receive the Russian army, and with the red flags, running with the bikes, with the horses, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a group from Polish army, where they were escaping and-- and to the east, they find out that the Germans left town, they came in. They came back to our town. And they started shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they shoot-- they-- I remember, at that time, one Jew, Lederman. He went out to close the-- the shutters from the window. He was shot. And then one Polish and like anti-Semite guy-- I know him-- was showing the-- the soldiers all the places. And they want to make trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in my grandfather's house lived a lady, by the name, Myalovitska, and was a dignified lady. Her husband was a lawyer, well-knowing. So we went to her, cry for help. So she went to the priests-- and to the priests, to beg them not to harm the people in town. And the priest went to the soldiers there. And they didn't do anything. They just retreat, escape to the forest. Who retreated to the

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Russian was three days, nobody. We didn't have any government in our town. And we were afraid. We were scared. But I think by around the same time, in 1939, on Yom Kippur, [BACKGROUND NOISE] one day airplanes, Russian airplanes came in. And they-- they throw out the leaflets that, we coming. And the following day, we got up in the morning, it was the whole city was screaming, dancing from joy, what with they came in. We had a government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we start to live under the Russian authority. Peoples remembered the Russians as primitive, [INAUDIBLE] in the revolution, because they called them the Borvesa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Borvesa means barefoot, because they-- when the communism fought against the Czar-- the Tsar, it was the farmers. They didn't have uniforms. And they just wear peasants farming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they called them, the Borvesa is coming. Not the Russian coming, the Borvesa is coming to our town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they came in, they tell everybody not to worry. Everybody that has business should conduct his business like usual.

speaker: interviewer
text: So like my father had a shoe store. I asked him, so what's [INAUDIBLE], where we'll buy shoes and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, us, we have enough. You'll go to Moscow, Kiev, we'll bring. Because before, the father used to bring from Warsaw, Radom, other towns, the materials. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But this wasn't so. They didn't have anything. After a couple months, they cleaned out the stores. Even we had their shoes, that were [INAUDIBLE] from-- from the Tsar, used to be their shoes. We-- supposed to be thrown out. Everybody was bought out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, what I'm trying to tell you, before, maybe. So my-- our house was attached to another house. And between two houses was room. The-- the roofs was like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was about two feet space between the houses. So in our attic, in the top, from the attic, we want to save.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the Russian [INAUDIBLE]. We had a lot of material, like leather and what we used to make shoes and everything, shoes, some clothes, so important items. The-- I lowered, myself. Well, I could fit in there. And loaded everything there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and what I have, for this, we could support ourself. Even in the black market, to sell everything, we could support ourself.
